target: data/wins.json WIN-010 — major rewrite of detail_verdict_text (entire field replacement) + targeted two-block patch to detail_evidence + tldr_verdict replacement. Addresses H1 (critical FSF factual error), H2 (Meza → Kim & Chang citation swap), H3 (stale Section 1.8 cross-reference), H4 (Chapman-qualitative advocate prong), H5 (similar-disc-radii phrase deletion), and two minor fixes (Kp range → Kp ≈ 3; refraction-cascade vocabulary).
patch_instructions:
Three edits to the WIN with id='010' in data/wins.json:
(1) detail_verdict_text: replace the ENTIRE field with replacement_detail_verdict_text (see below).
(2) detail_evidence: find the unique substring marked detail_evidence_block_patch.before and replace it with detail_evidence_block_patch.after (one-occurrence match, verified 2026-04-22).
(3) detail_evidence: find the unique Kp substring detail_evidence_kp_patch.before and replace with detail_evidence_kp_patch.after (one-occurrence match, verified 2026-04-22).
(4) tldr_verdict: replace the ENTIRE field with replacement_tldr_verdict.
All other WIN-010 fields (detail_claim, detail_extra, tldr_evidence, verdict, verdict_history, code_analysis, finding, claim) are unchanged.
replacement_detail_verdict_text: Non-discriminating: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054), whose 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble reproduces the systematic Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° — predicts the observed 2017 BOU signal magnitude and geometry within measurement uncertainty without any dome parameters. The mechanism is straightforward: the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq (solar quiet) current system. The resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. The dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations; it simply observes that the signal exists and claims it. The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses the BOU 2017 −10.9 nT value as both a claimed WIN and the baseline input for future predictions — the same number counted twice, once as evidence and once as a parameter. In science, training data cannot serve as test data. Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the field-strength factor genuinely does equal H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed). The dome is telling the truth about its geometric derivation — that piece of the argument should be granted. Being right about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation itself, not derived from H(r), n(r), or any other dome parameter; every other station's delta_Z is an observed BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio. Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity and current-system parameters once against the ionospheric-physics literature and then predict future, independent events; the dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration. The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form, so matching one parametrization to 0.1% does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function. The Kp precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard: the 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp ≈ 3 (unsettled — NOAA's G-scale geomagnetic-disturbance threshold begins at Kp 5 / G1), yet the dome's monitor.py imposes Kp < 2 for 2026 eclipse validation. The model accepts an unsettled-day observation as a confirmed WIN but requires quiet-day conditions for its own future tests. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from the public INTERMAGNET BOU archive, available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values that scaling feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles — angles that travel through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere. Every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination, so even the one genuine derivation the dome has produced anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are. The scaling is internally consistent given the coordinates; the coordinates themselves are compromised by the dome's own refraction fix. This WIN now sits in front of a clean external test. The dome publishes specific per-station delta_Z predictions for the 2026-08-12 European eclipse (EBR −10.7, SPT −9.8, ESK −10.3, LER −9.5, HAD −12.8, NGK −6.7, SNK −5.0, CLF −10.1, COI −10.1 nT). Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 observations track FSF more tightly than they track eclipse shadow coverage (Besselian-elements geometry, the standard-model prediction). Kim & Chang's ensemble shows the Y-component and X/Z/F responses track shadow geometry at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, not disc-radius geometry. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if delta_Z tracks shadow coverage more tightly than FSF, the dome's scaling is post-hoc; if FSF beats coverage on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining. Note: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1933; Kim & Chang 2018) as their standard-physics refutation. Count inflation by itself does not refute any individual claim, but is relevant context when the dome cites 69 confirmed predictions — three of those claims share one mechanism.

detail_evidence_block_patch.before: Additionally, the dome's 2026 eclipse predictions use a 'field strength factor' (FSF) for each station — claimed to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' — but FSF values range from 0.642 to 2.075 across stations, a 3.2× spread across ~32° of European latitude (from the Canary Islands at 28.3°N through Scandinavia). Since H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on disc radius (not geographic latitude), dome geometry cannot produce this variation among stations at similar disc radii. The FSF spread indicates post-hoc empirical fitting to observed eclipse data, not first-principles derivation from dome parameters.
detail_evidence_block_patch.after: Additionally, the dome's 2026 eclipse predictions use a 'field strength factor' (FSF) for each station — claimed to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry'. Direct computation confirms that claim: solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and normalizing to Boulder reproduces all nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven stations and within 1.8% at the worst, with Pearson r = 0.9999. The dome's geometric scaling between stations is real. What the scaling does not provide is the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT), which is imported from the 2017 observation itself — making every other station's predicted delta_Z an observed number scaled by a geometric ratio, not a prediction of the signal from dome parameters.

detail_evidence_kp_patch.before: recorded during Kp = 3–4 (unsettled conditions — NOAA G-scale geomagnetic disturbance begins at Kp 5 / G1)
detail_evidence_kp_patch.after: recorded at Kp ≈ 3 (unsettled — NOAA's G-scale geomagnetic-disturbance threshold begins at Kp 5 / G1)

current_tldr_verdict: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, known for 90+ years, predicts this signal without dome parameters. The dome adds no equation, no derivation — just observes a known phenomenon and claims credit. The 2017 baseline also serves as the input for 2026 predictions, making the 2017 baseline training data serving as its own test.
replacement_tldr_verdict: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, documented since 1933 and quantitatively confirmed by Kim & Chang (2018) across 39 eclipses, predicts this signal without dome parameters. The dome does genuinely derive a station-to-station scaling (FSF = H(r)/r) but it anchors that scaling to the 2017 Boulder observation itself, reusing the same −10.9 nT value as both confirmed WIN and 2026 baseline — a ratio between stations is not a prediction of the signal, and the absolute magnitude is still borrowed from the data.

holes_addressed.hole: H1 (critical) — ISS-1390 — FSF empirical-fitting factual error
holes_addressed.description: Old detail_verdict_text claimed "FSF values are empirically fitted to observed eclipse data, not derived from dome parameters." Direct numerical verification (curmudgeon c5 and independent recompute here) shows FSF = (H(r)/r)/(H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values to <0.1% at 7 of 9 stations, <1.8% worst case, Pearson r = 0.9999. The FSF derivation the dome claims is genuine.
holes_addressed.fix: Paragraph 2 now explicitly credits FSF = H(r)/r as a real geometric derivation with numerical verification (0.1%/1.8%/r=0.9999). Paragraph 3 pivots to the two arguments that actually hold: (a) a ratio between stations is not a prediction of the absolute BOU magnitude, which is imported from the 2017 observation; (b) any smooth H(r) plus the r·tan(θ)=H(r) convention produces the same kind of scaling, so the 0.1% match does not uniquely identify dome geometry vs any alternative disc-geometry height-function model. The training-as-test critique is preserved and strengthened (reframed as in-sample vs out-of-sample evaluation).
holes_addressed.inverts: Position: was "FSF is empirical fitting, not derivation"; now "FSF really is geometric scaling, but scaling is not a magnitude prediction and the anchor is still the observation." The Advocate-mode defense (defense_survives=5) collapses because the review now concedes the part the defender could verify with 5 lines of Python.

holes_addressed.hole: H2 (major) — ISS-1391 — Meza (2021) citation contradicts WIN-068
holes_addressed.description: WIN-010 cited Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics, doi:10.1029/2020JA028746) as the quantitative anchor; WIN-068 removed the exact same citation on 2026-04-16 as "unverifiable, 2 cycles unresolved." A reader cross-checking the two panels finds the project simultaneously asserting and disavowing the same reference. Also DOI was never end-to-end verified.
holes_addressed.fix: Meza swapped for Kim & Chang (2018, Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054), which is already verified and cited in WIN-068. One verified anchor now covers WIN-010/025/068. Triple-count note in paragraph 8 updated to name both Chapman 1933 and Kim & Chang 2018 as the shared standard-physics anchor.

holes_addressed.description: Old paragraph referenced "The dome's refraction cascade (see Section 1.8)" and summarized it as "sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate." Section 1.8 was renamed to "The Coordinate Collapse: One Fix Destroys the Map" on 2026-04-12 per HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001; the current framing is Polaris-angle contamination of the dome coordinate system, not seasonal sun indeterminacy. Old summary also baked in the now-falsified "FSF is empirical" framing.
holes_addressed.fix: Paragraph 6 fully rewritten: cites current Section 1.8 title ("The Coordinate Collapse"), uses current framing (Polaris-angle contamination through n(r) medium, 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere), and surgically threads the needle by accepting FSF = H(r)/r as geometric scaling while arguing every r value the scaling feeds on is itself contaminated by the n(r) medium. "Refraction cascade" vocabulary removed throughout (minor hole resolved in same pass).

holes_addressed.description: Advocate Mode Prong A: a defender could object "Chapman 1933 is qualitative; you are smuggling in modern Sq models that have free parameters." c5 text still leaned only on Meza — and Meza is now unverified per H2. Kim & Chang (2018) is peer-reviewed, real, quantitative, and removes the "Chapman is qualitative" handle.
holes_addressed.fix: Closed automatically with the Meza → Kim & Chang swap. Paragraph 1 now cites both Chapman (1933) AND Kim & Chang (2018) with the 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble and the magnetic-latitude 30°–50° pattern explicitly named — peer-reviewed modern quantitative confirmation of the Chapman framework, not a free-parameter rescue.

holes_addressed.hole: H5 (moderate) — ISS-1394 — "similar disc radii" phrase dead on arrival
holes_addressed.description: Advocate Mode Prong B: a defender can compute r values from the dome's own formula and show stations are NOT at similar disc radii (SNK r=7021 km vs LER r=3330 km; full range spans 3330–7021 km). FSF correlates with H(r)/r at r=0.9999. There is no station pair with "similar r but different FSF." The argument is factually wrong.
holes_addressed.fix: Phrase "among stations at similar disc radii" deleted from both detail_verdict_text and detail_evidence (both occurrences found, both patched). Argument pivots to the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude bite (H1 fix) and the scaling-shape-is-generic bite (any monotonic H(r) works), which do not depend on a similar-r claim.

holes_addressed.description: Three-hour Kp indices give a single value per 3-hour window; "3–4" reads as uncertainty about the exact value. Asking "which is it" is a debating handle a defender can pick up.
holes_addressed.fix: Replaced with "Kp ≈ 3" in both detail_verdict_text and detail_evidence. Conservative choice (quieter neighbor — the asymmetric-standard argument still holds), removes the precision-of-source debating handle.

holes_addressed.description: c5 review flagged two stronger arguments not yet in the reader-facing text: (a) the 2026-08-12 nine-station eclipse is a concrete upcoming out-of-sample test; (b) Kim & Chang's shadow-geometry pattern (mag-lat 30°–50° Y-component increase) is directly checkable against FSF predictions post-eclipse.
holes_addressed.fix: Paragraph 7 (new) adds the "2026 clean external test" framing with the exact nine per-station delta_Z predictions (EBR −10.7 through COI −10.1), names the critical comparison (FSF tightness vs shadow-coverage tightness), references Kim & Chang's shadow-geometry pattern as the alternative prediction, and commits to updating this WIN when 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives. Turns a soft "non-discriminating" into a concrete upcoming test window.

new_evidence_added: Independent numerical verification of FSF = H(r)/r scaling across all nine dome-published stations; match to <0.1% at 7 of 9, <1.8% worst; Pearson r = 0.9999 (confirms c5 curmudgeon computation from an independent node session).
Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) — peer-reviewed 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble study, already verified in WIN-068; now the shared anchor across WIN-010/025/068.

Current Section 1.8 ("The Coordinate Collapse") framing integrated into WIN-010: Polaris-angle contamination through n(r) medium (300–6,000× Earth's atmosphere) as the bridge between coordinate system and FSF.
Per-station 2026-08-12 delta_Z predictions (nine stations, exact nT values from raw-text/03-predictions.txt) surfaced explicitly with commitment to post-eclipse WIN update.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome author or a defender could argue that although H(r)/r is a valid ratio scaling, the dome's H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) functional form is itself derived from first principles (firmament cavity geometry), not arbitrary — so the scaling shape IS uniquely dome-geometric, not "any monotonic H(r)".
anticipated_objections.response: The exponential functional form H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) has two fitted parameters (amplitude 8537 and decay length 8619) and no independent first-principles derivation in raw-text/05-model.txt — the values are chosen to fit observed sun altitude (5,733 km) at the BOU disc radius together with other calibrated geometric targets. Replacing exp(−r/8619) with any competitor fitted to the same calibration targets (Gaussian exp(−r²/L²), linear H₀ − kr, power-law, etc.) produces nearly identical ratios over the 3330–7021 km range actually sampled by the nine stations, because all smooth monotonic H(r) profiles look similar over a 2× r-range when normalized to a single anchor. The 0.1% match is to H(r)/r; it is not a differential test against alternative height functions.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender could argue the pivot from "empirical fitting" to "scaling-without-anchor" is a retreat — the review was wrong once, why trust the new framing?
anticipated_objections.response: Concession on one point strengthens, not weakens, the rest. The review missed a five-line Python check at c3/c4 and a c5 lens-C verification caught it; that is the process working. The new framing survives all three advocate-mode prongs the curmudgeon enumerated (Chapman qualitative, similar-disc-radii, empirical-tuning) simultaneously, and the training-as-test + post-hoc + triple-count pillars are untouched. The corrected text also pre-commits to a concrete 2026-08-12 out-of-sample test — the first time this WIN has had a forward-looking discriminator. If FSF beats shadow coverage on clean 2026 data, the review will update.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender could argue "the BOU magnitude isn't adopted from 2017 — standard Sq modeling also predicts it from first principles, so the dome borrowing the observed value is no worse than standard practice."
anticipated_objections.response: Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity/current-system parameters once against a broad ionospheric-physics literature (ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, climatologies) — none of which are fitted to the specific eclipse magnetic signal being predicted — and then predict future, independent eclipses out-of-sample. The dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation. The asymmetry is between one-time calibration against independent reference data (standard practice) and reusing the same event as both training and test (what the dome does). Paragraph 3 of the new detail_verdict_text states this distinction explicitly.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender could argue the Section 1.8 bridge is strained — the Polaris-angle contamination affects latitude-to-r conversion but the dome's FSF values are pegged to the claimed r values, so if anything the contamination is internally absorbed.
anticipated_objections.response: That is the correct diagnosis, and it is a problem for the dome, not a defense. If the r values in the FSF table are contaminated, then the "geometric derivation" the dome celebrates (FSF = H(r)/r) is derivation on contaminated coordinates. The dome cannot both claim the r values are physical disc radii and use them to validate a geometric scaling against observed magnetic data — one of those roles is doing the work, the other is borrowing the answer. This is the same "coordinates measured through the medium the coordinates describe" circularity Section 1.8 documents.

kernel_of_truth: The dome is genuinely correct that (a) the 2017 BOU −10.9 nT observation exists, is recorded at eclipse maximum, and fits the Chapman ionospheric Sq framework; (b) FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) is a clean geometric scaling that reproduces the published FSF table to Pearson r = 0.9999 — a legitimate derivation from the dome's own V12 coordinate equations. We were wrong to describe it as "empirical fitting" for three review cycles. What the dome has NOT done is derive the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor; that number is imported from the 2017 observation itself, so every station's predicted delta_Z is an observed number scaled by a geometric ratio, not a magnitude prediction from dome parameters. The scaling is correct; the scaling is also not a prediction.
newsworthy: Dome Review Corrects Itself: FSF Scaling Is Real Geometry After All — But the Anchor Is Still Borrowed

cross_win_consistency_flag.issue: WIN-068 detail_verdict_text contains the same "consistent with empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation from H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)" claim that we are now retracting in WIN-010. The same Pearson-r=0.9999 recomputation applies. Flagging for decider: the triple-count cluster needs a follow-up issue/EXP to propagate the FSF correction into WIN-068 (and possibly WIN-025 if it makes the same claim).
cross_win_consistency_flag.recommended_action: Decider: open a new issue "WIN-068 FSF empirical-fitting claim inherits WIN-010 c5 factual error" and assign an EXP for parallel rewrite. Do NOT expand EXP-220 scope to include WIN-068 — keep EXP-220 focused and let the cluster-wide propagation be its own tracked item.

out_of_scope_deliberately: Kp value precise sourcing with URL link (curmudgeon minor hole): softened to "Kp ≈ 3" per curmudgeon option (b), which closes the debating handle without requiring a new citation.
WIN-068 / WIN-025 parallel FSF correction: flagged for decider as a separate follow-up (see cross_win_consistency_flag above).

Advocate Prong C (symmetric empirical tuning): the "standard Sq models also tune parameters" objection is addressed directly in paragraph 3 of the new detail_verdict_text (in-sample vs out-of-sample distinction) and in anticipated_objections above. No separate patch needed.

detail_claim, detail_extra, tldr_evidence, verdict, finding, verdict_history: unchanged. The verdict "Std Model Explains" still holds — Chapman + Kim & Chang predict the magnitude; BOU is training-test; post-hoc ordering; triple-count; coordinate-collapse contamination. Only the text supporting the verdict changes.
